questions.prompt: Grade 9 Yearlong Review · Belief + Rule · $300: Monotheistic belief system founded by Muhammad.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.explanation: Islam (founded 622 CE) is the monotheistic religion revealed to the Prophet Muhammad in Arabia, based on the Five Pillars: testimony of faith (shahada), prayer (salat), charity (zakat), fasting during Ramadan (sawm), and pilgrimage to Mecca (hajj). Islam spread rapidly from Arabia across the Middle East, North Africa, Spain, and Central Asia within a century of Muhammad's death, creating a vast civilization. By 750 CE the Abbasid Caliphate stretched from Spain to Central Asia.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.prompt: Grade 9 Yearlong Review · Belief + Rule · $400: Chinese philosophy stressing order, duty, and family roles.
questions.explanation: Confucianism, based on the teachings of Kong Qiu (Confucius, 551-479 BCE), is a Chinese philosophical system emphasizing proper social relationships, filial piety (respect for parents and ancestors), education, and good governance through virtuous rulers. It structured Chinese society around five relationships (ruler-subject, parent-child, husband-wife, elder-younger, friend-friend) and justified the civil service examination system that governed China for 1,300 years.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.prompt: Grade 9 Yearlong Review · Belief + Rule · $500: Medieval wars tied to religion and control of the Holy Land.
questions.explanation: The Crusades (1096-1291) were a series of religious wars launched by Catholic Europe to capture Jerusalem and the Holy Land from Muslim rulers, motivated by the Seljuk Turks' restriction of Christian pilgrimages. Although the First Crusade briefly captured Jerusalem in 1099, most Crusades failed militarily and European control of Jerusalem lasted less than a century. They deepened Christian-Muslim hostility but opened trade routes and brought Arab science and luxury goods to Europe.

questions.explanation: Athens (5th century BCE) was the birthplace of Western democratic concepts under leaders like Cleisthenes and Pericles, who extended political participation to male citizens in the assembly, jury courts, and elected offices. The Athenian agora was both a marketplace and the site of political debate. However, Athenian democracy excluded women, enslaved people (who were 30-40% of the population), and resident non-citizens from political participation.

questions.explanation: The Pax Romana (27 BCE-180 CE) was roughly 200 years of relative peace and stability across the Roman Empire under the first emperors. It enabled trade networks stretching from Britain to Mesopotamia, Roman law to spread across Europe, and a population of 50-70 million to flourish under unified administration. Roads, aqueducts, and the common Latin language integrated diverse peoples into a shared Roman civilization.

questions.prompt: Grade 9 Yearlong Review · Classical + Trade · $300: Classical Chinese dynasty known for bureaucracy and Confucian government.

questions.explanation: The Han Dynasty (206 BCE-220 CE) was China's golden age of consolidation, establishing Confucian civil service examinations, expanding the Silk Road trade network, and creating a centralized bureaucratic state that became the model for all subsequent Chinese dynasties. Han China and the contemporary Roman Empire were the two largest empires on earth, each controlling roughly 30% of world population. Han China's influence on East Asian culture parallels Rome's influence on the West.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.prompt: Grade 9 Yearlong Review · Classical + Trade · $400: Trade routes connecting East Asia, Central Asia, the Middle East, and Europe.
questions.explanation: The Silk Roads were a network of overland and maritime trade routes connecting China, Central Asia, India, the Middle East, and Europe, carrying silk, spices, paper, gunpowder, and porcelain eastward and westward. More than goods, the Silk Roads transmitted religions (Buddhism, Islam, Christianity), technologies (paper, printing, compass), and diseases (the Black Death) across Afro-Eurasia. Regents questions consistently test the Silk Roads as the prime example of cultural diffusion.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.prompt: Grade 9 Yearlong Review · Classical + Trade · $500: Mali ruler whose pilgrimage showed West African wealth.
questions.explanation: Mansa Musa (r.1312-1337) was the ruler of the Mali Empire whose famous 1324-25 hajj to Mecca with 60,000 attendants and 12,000 enslaved servants distributed so much gold across Egypt and the Middle East that it caused inflation lasting over a decade. His journey put Mali on European maps for the first time and demonstrated the enormous wealth generated by West Africa's gold-salt trade. He remains the wealthiest person in recorded history by modern estimates.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

Based at Constantinople (modern Istanbul), it preserved Roman law, Greek language, and Orthodox Christianity, transmitting classical learning to the Islamic world and later to Renaissance Europe. Justinian's Code (Corpus Juris Civilis) is its most lasting legacy and forms the basis of most European legal systems today.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

After devastating conquests (including the sack of Baghdad in 1258 ending the Abbasid Caliphate), the Mongols established the Pax Mongolica, a period of enforced peace that reopened and secured the Silk Roads. Marco Polo traveled these routes, and the Black Death also traveled them westward.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

At its peak under Suleiman the Magnificent (r.1520-1566), it stretched from Hungary to Yemen. As one of history's gunpowder empires, it used cannon technology to conquer and hold a vast territory across three continents.

Ming emperors used the civil service examination to select officials and the tribute system to manage relations with neighboring states. The dynasty's eventual turn inward contrasted sharply with Europe's simultaneous age of exploration.

questions.prompt: Grade 9 Yearlong Review · Empires + States · $500: Andean empire known for roads, quipu, and state labor.

It was destroyed by 168 Spanish soldiers under Pizarro in 1532-1533, aided by smallpox and a civil war between rival princes. The Inca remain the Regents' go-to example of a sophisticated non-literate civilization.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.explanation: The Scientific Revolution (c.1543-1687) overturned centuries of Church-sanctioned ancient authority through systematic observation and mathematical reasoning. Copernicus proposed a sun-centered solar system; Galileo confirmed it with telescope observations; Newton unified terrestrial and celestial mechanics with his law of universal gravitation. This revolution in how Europeans understood nature directly inspired Enlightenment thinkers to apply the same rational methods to human society and government.

questions.prompt: Grade 9 Yearlong Review · Early Modern Change · $400: Exchange of plants, animals, diseases, and peoples across hemispheres.

questions.explanation: The Columbian Exchange was the vast two-way transfer of plants, animals, diseases, and people between the Eastern and Western Hemispheres after Columbus's 1492 voyage. New World crops (corn, potatoes, tomatoes) eventually doubled Europe's food supply and drove population growth; Old World diseases (smallpox, measles) killed an estimated 50-90% of indigenous American populations. No event transformed both hemispheres more rapidly or profoundly.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.explanation: The Middle Passage was the trans-Atlantic leg of the triangular trade in which an estimated 12-15 million enslaved Africans were transported from West Africa to the Americas, with 1.5-2 million dying at sea from disease, abuse, and despair. Enslaved people were packed into ships' holds in conditions of extreme brutality for voyages of 6-12 weeks. It represents one of history's greatest crimes against humanity and shaped the demographics, economics, and culture of the entire Atlantic world.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.prompt: Grade 9 Yearlong Review · Yearlong Global Pattern · $700: This process explains how religions, technologies, crops, and ideas spread as societies interact.

questions.topic: Grade 9 Yearlong Review · Yearlong Global Pattern
questions.explanation: Cultural diffusion is the spread of ideas, technologies, religions, artistic styles, and practices from one culture to another through trade, conquest, migration, or intermarriage. The entire Global 9 course is built on cultural diffusion: Buddhism spreading along the Silk Road, Islam following Arab traders to East Africa and Southeast Asia, the printing press spreading Reformation ideas across Europe, and the Columbian Exchange transforming both hemispheres. Regents questions regularly ask students to identify examples of cultural diffusion and explain its effects.
